claims.claim: On 13 May 2026, the Federal Court adjourned former Felda chairman Isa Samad's conviction review.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026, 'Federal Court Adjourns Isa Samad's Leave Application Pending CRT Access', https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2522288; The Star, 13 May 2026, 'Federal Court defers Isa Samad review bid, allows access to court transcripts', https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2026/05/13/federal-court-defers-isa-samad-review-bid-allows-access-to-court-transcripts
claims.correction: Accurate. The proceeding was described as a leave application under Rule 137 to review the Federal Court's earlier decision reinstating the conviction and sentence.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue found. Public reporting available up to 17 May 2026 still supports this.

claims.claim: The defence counsel and the deputy public prosecutor gave conflicting accounts of what happened on 10 February.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2522288; The Star, 13 May 2026; The Edge Malaysia, 13 May 2026, https://theedgemalaysia.com/node/803361
claims.correction: Accurate as an attribution claim. Public sources verify the defence and prosecution disagreed over whether the defence had been invited or given the opportunity to address merits and mitigation. The public sources do not verify which account is factually correct.

claims.claim: The bench ordered the courtroom audio released to both sides.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Star, 13 May 2026; The Edge Malaysia, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Accurate, but the precise term used in reports is Court Recording and Transcription, or CRT, for the open court proceedings on 5 February and 10 February 2026. The order appears to supply access to the parties, not to publish the audio publicly.

claims.claim: On 13 May, the apex court adjourned Isa Samad's review and ordered the courtroom audio released to both sides.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Vibes, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Accurate. Replace 'audio released' with 'CRT access allowed for both parties' if tighter legal wording is preferred.

claims.claim: The defence says the 10 February bench never invited it to submit on the merits.
claims.source: The Star, 13 May 2026; Bernama, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Accurate as a statement of the defence's position. It should not be presented as proof that no invitation occurred unless the CRT or transcript confirms it.

claims.claim: The prosecution says the bench did invite the defence to submit, and the defence declined.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Star, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Accurate as an attribution claim. The public reports confirm the prosecution's position, but the underlying procedural fact remains unresolved without the CRT or transcript.

claims.claim: Three courts heard the same nine corruption charges and reached three different conclusions: convict 2021, acquit 2024, convict again 2026.
claims.source: Malay Mail, 3 February 2021; Court of Appeal Broad Grounds of Judgment, Mohd Isa Abdul Samad v Public Prosecutor, 6 March 2024, https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-03/W-05%28SH%29-28-02-2021.pdf; Federal Court judgment text, Public Prosecutor v Mohd Isa Abdul Samad, 10 February 2026, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2026_12.html
claims.correction: Accurate in substance. For precision, say the Federal Court restored the High Court conviction rather than 'convicted again' as if conducting a fresh trial.

claims.claim: Each ruling concerned RM3.09 million in alleged kickbacks tied to Felda's RM160 million purchase of Merdeka Palace Hotel in Kuching in 2015.
claims.source: Federal Court judgment text, Public Prosecutor v Mohd Isa Abdul Samad, 10 February 2026, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2026_12.html; Bernama, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: The RM3.09 million and RM160 million figures are supported. However, the judgment frames the purchase as FICSB's purchase of Merdeka Palace Hotel and Suites from GAPSB, with board approval and acceptance steps in 2014, while alleged payments ran from 2014 to 2015. Replace with: 'Each ruling concerned RM3.09 million in alleged gratification linked to FICSB's RM160 million purchase of Merdeka Palace Hotel and Suites in Kuching, approved in 2014, with alleged payments spanning 2014 to 2015.'

claims.claim: Defence counsel M Athimulan says the bench never invited him to submit on the merits. DPP Afzainizam Abdul Aziz says it did, and Athimulan declined.
claims.source: The Star, 13 May 2026; Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Vibes, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Accurate as reported positions. Keep the wording as 'says' or 'contends' and avoid converting either account into a factual finding.

claims.claim: Both stand on the record.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Star, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: If 'record' means public media reports of statements made in open court, the claim is broadly supportable. If it implies both positions are already established in the formal court record or CRT, that is not publicly verified. Safer wording: 'Both positions were reported as statements made in open court.'

claims.claim: The Chief Judge of Malaya ordered the courtroom audio of 5 and 10 February released to both sides on 13 May.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Star, 13 May 2026; The Edge Malaysia, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Accurate. The reports identify Chief Judge of Malaya Hashim Hamzah as chair of the panel and refer to CRT access for the proceedings on 5 and 10 February 2026.

claims.claim: Rule 137 reviews of an apex-court ruling are granted only in rare circumstances: lack of quorum, established bias, or integrity critically undermined.
claims.source: Rules of the Federal Court 1995, Rule 137, P.U.(A) 376/1995; Federal Court judgment, Najib Razak review, 2023, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_12.html
claims.correction: The rare-and-exceptional standard is broadly correct, but the formulation is too narrow and the word 'only' overstates the position. Federal Court review jurisdiction is not a merits appeal and is reserved for exceptional circumstances to prevent injustice or abuse of process. Recognised categories include quorum failure, procedural unfairness or breach of natural justice, denial of the right to be heard or right to counsel, bias, and situations where the integrity of the earlier decision is critically undermined.

claims.claim: Najib Razak's Rule 137 bid to revisit his SRC conviction was dismissed 4-1 on 31 March 2023.
claims.source: Federal Court judgment, Najib Razak review, 2023, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_12.html; Malaysian Bar, 7 April 2023, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/legal-and-general-news/legal-news/najib-fails-to-get-second-review-of-src-conviction-and-sentence-federal-court-rules
claims.correction: Accurate. Some news reports were published on 30 March 2023 depending on timing, but the 31 March 2023 date is supported in legal reporting and subsequent summaries.

claims.claim: The dissenting judge in Najib Razak's Rule 137 review cited breach of natural justice.
claims.source: Federal Court judgment, Najib Razak review, 2023, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2023_12.html
claims.correction: Accurate. The dissenting judgment framed the issue around the right to a fair hearing, effective counsel, adjournment refusal, and breach of natural justice.

claims.claim: When two officers of the court give irreconcilable accounts of what the apex court said in open session, the question is not who is right. It is why the routine record is not the record.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Edge Malaysia, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: This is an editorial inference, not a factual claim. It is grounded in the CRT-access order, but 'why the routine record is not the record' may overstate the issue because the court did not find that the official record failed; it allowed CRT access to resolve a dispute. Safer wording: 'The immediate procedural question is whether the CRT resolves the parties' conflicting accounts of what occurred in open court.'

claims.claim: The Federal Court has now ordered the audio of two earlier hearings released so both sides, and the next bench, can hear what the previous bench said.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Star, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: The order for both parties to access CRT is verified. The statement that 'the next bench' can hear it is a reasonable inference, but I did not find a public source saying the court expressly ordered CRT access for the next bench's use.

claims.claim: The fourth round is not about whether Isa is guilty. It is about whether the third round was heard.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; Rule 137, Rules of the Federal Court 1995
claims.correction: This is a fair editorial shorthand if presented as analysis, because Rule 137 review is procedural and exceptional rather than a fresh merits appeal. However, 'whether the third round was heard' should be phrased carefully: the public dispute is whether the defence was invited or given proper opportunity to address merits and mitigation, not whether the entire Federal Court appeal was unheard.

claims.claim: Isa Samad's High Court conviction on 3 February 2021 resulted in six years' jail and RM15.45 million fine on nine counts.
claims.source: Malay Mail, 3 February 2021; Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026
claims.correction: Accurate. Be careful with default imprisonment wording, because public reports vary between two years and a cumulative 18 years depending on how the default sentences are described.

claims.claim: The Court of Appeal unanimously acquitted Isa Samad on 6 March 2024.
claims.source: Court of Appeal Broad Grounds of Judgment, 6 March 2024; The Star, 6 March 2024
claims.correction: Accurate.

claims.claim: The Federal Court reinstated Isa Samad's conviction and sentence on 10 February 2026.
claims.source: Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026; Bernama, 10 February 2026
claims.correction: Accurate. The Federal Court allowed the prosecution's appeal, set aside the Court of Appeal's acquittal, and restored the High Court conviction and sentence.

claims.claim: The Federal Court's 10 February 2026 ruling invoked the MACC Act section 50(1) presumption.
claims.source: Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026; MACC Act 2009 official text, https://www.sprm.gov.my/admin/uploads_publication/akta-694-bi-01092019-001-145.pdf
claims.correction: Accurate. The Federal Court held that the Court of Appeal had misdirected itself on the operation of the section 50(1) presumption.

claims.claim: The substantive charges have been variously summarised as corrupt acceptance under MACC Act sections 16 and 17(a).
claims.source: Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026; MACC Act 2009, sections 16, 17, 24 and 50
claims.correction: The case record identifies the nine corruption charges as charges under section 16(a)(A) of the MACC Act 2009, punishable under section 24(1). Section 50(1) is the evidential presumption. I did not verify section 17(a) as the charge section in this case. Remove section 17(a) unless a charge sheet or judgment passage specifically supports it.

claims.claim: The RM15.45 million fine reflects five times the RM3.09 million alleged gratification amount.
claims.source: Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026; MACC Act 2009, section 24(1)
claims.correction: Accurate. Section 24(1) provides for a fine of not less than five times the sum or value of gratification, where capable of valuation, or RM10,000, whichever is higher.

claims.claim: The new substantive hearing date for the Rule 137 review was not in the public reporting accessed at brief drafting.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Star, 13 May 2026
claims.correction: Public reports verified that case management was fixed for 14 May 2026. I did not locate a later public report, up to 17 May 2026 searches, giving the next substantive hearing date. This should remain an information gap rather than a factual assertion about the court diary.
claims.stale_check: This is time-sensitive. It may become stale if a later court date is reported after 17 May 2026.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Rule 137 reviews of an apex-court ruling are granted only in rare circumstances: lack of quorum, established bias, or integrity critically undermined.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The rare-and-exceptional framing is broadly right, but the list is too narrow and the word 'only' is too strong. Natural justice and fair-hearing failures are central recognised grounds and directly relevant to Isa Samad's review argument.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Court judgment, Najib Razak review, 2023; Rules of the Federal Court 1995, Rule 137

cardinal_sin_flags.why: The Federal Court judgment identifies Isa Samad's corruption charges as section 16(a)(A), punishable under section 24(1), with section 50(1) used as the statutory presumption. I did not verify section 17(a) as part of the charge basis for this case.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026; MACC Act 2009 official text

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Felda's RM160 million purchase of Merdeka Palace Hotel in Kuching in 2015
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The amount and hotel are correct, but the judgment frames the purchaser as FICSB and shows the board approval and acceptance steps occurring in 2014, while the alleged gratification payments spanned 2014 to 2015. Calling it 'Felda's 2015 purchase' compresses the entity and timeline.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: the question is not who is right. It is why the routine record is not the record.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The court allowed CRT access because there was a dispute over what happened in open court. Public sources do not establish that the routine record failed or that the court itself treated the ordinary record as inadequate beyond needing CRT access to resolve the dispute.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Bernama, 13 May 2026; The Edge Malaysia, 13 May 2026

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: the next substantive hearing date ... is not in the public reporting at brief drafting
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This appears true based on available searches up to 17 May 2026, but it is inherently time-sensitive and depends on the set of sources checked. It should be kept as a live verification item rather than treated as settled.

omitted_facts: Federal Court judgment text for 10 February 2026 states prosecution appeal allowed because Court of Appeal misapplied MACC Act s.50(1) presumption and prosecution proved ingredients of all nine s.16(a)(A) charges beyond reasonable doubt. Source: https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2026_12.html

Court of Appeal Broad Grounds explain the 2024 acquittal on the basis that the prosecution had presented conflicting versions or narratives and that ambiguity should be resolved in favour of the accused. Source: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-03/W-05%28SH%29-28-02-2021.pdf
Purchase timeline: FICSB board approval around April 2014 and acceptance by GAPSB in May 2014, alleged payments across 2014 to 2015. Source: Federal Court judgment text, 10 February 2026
Charge section is s.16(a)(A) of MACC Act 2009, punishable under s.24(1), with s.50(1) as the presumption provision. s.17(a) should not be listed for this case.
The RM15.45 million fine is intelligible as five times RM3.09 million under MACC Act s.24(1).

Default imprisonment for non-payment of the fine: some reports refer to a two-year default term, others to a cumulative 18-year default. Verify directly from sentencing order or judgment for any future version.
No public source up to 17 May 2026 confirms the post-case-management substantive hearing date for the Rule 137 review.
source_assessment: The analysis uses a strong spread of Malaysian legal and news sources. The main source weakness is that the central procedural dispute about whether the defence was invited to submit on 10 February 2026 ultimately depends on CRT or transcript evidence that is not publicly reproduced in the sources checked. The Rule 137 legal standard leans partly on practitioner commentary; it should be anchored more directly to Federal Court judgments such as the Najib review and the text of Rule 137 itself. The analysis should also clean up the MACC Act section references and the FICSB/Felda purchase timeline.
overall_assessment: The draft is substantially reliable on the core structural facts: High Court conviction in 2021, Court of Appeal acquittal in 2024, Federal Court reinstatement in 2026, Rule 137 review filing, 13 May 2026 adjournment, CRT access order, and the reported dispute between defence and prosecution. The main weaknesses are not fatal but should be corrected before publication: the Rule 137 standard is too narrowly stated, the MACC Act section list wrongly or at least unverifiably includes section 17(a), the purchase should be described as FICSB's RM160 million MPHS transaction rather than simply Felda's 2015 purchase, and all claims about what actually happened on 10 February must remain framed as competing assertions unless the CRT or transcript is obtained. With those corrections, the piece can safely run as a process-focused analysis rather than a merits claim about Isa Samad's guilt or the judges' conduct.
